system_context.business_goals: Provide a fully automated test environment for Prolog programs.

architectures.name: PROTest II
architectures.description: An interactive test environment for Prolog programs that partially automates test execution, test development, and test measurement.

key_components.responsibilities: Generates test inputs based on test coverage.
Analyzes instrumented source code to determine clause coverage.

architectures.data_flow: The system follows a sequential pipeline where the Structure Checker parses source code, the Input Generator creates test cases, the Test Driver executes them, the Coverage Analyzer measures logic paths, and the Report Generator summarizes the results.
architectural_constraints: Strictly bound to Prolog syntax and execution semantics
Requires source code instrumentation for coverage measurement
architectures.notes: The architecture can easily accommodate new reporting formats or input generation algorithms. However, it cannot easily accommodate non-logic programming languages or real-time black-box testing without significant modifications to the parsing and execution components.

scenario_elicitation.elicitation_method: AI-Driven Brainstorming based on System Context

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user provides a Prolog source file to the system; the system interacts with the user to select specific predicates, generates test inputs, and displays real-time coverage metrics via the interface.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Directly supported by the current interactive architecture and component set.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Ensures the system meets the core requirement of being an interactive test environment for specific Prolog modules.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer initiates a 'headless' mode where the system automatically parses a directory of Prolog files, generates inputs for all exported predicates, executes them, and saves a JSON report without manual prompts.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect fit; requires a new automation controller or CLI wrapper to bypass the current interactive user-input loops.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Addresses the stakeholder need for a 'fully automated' environment, moving beyond 'partial' automation.

scenarios.scenario_text: A maintainer modifies the Structure Checker to support a new Prolog dialect (e.g., SWI-Prolog specific extensions) within two person-days, ensuring the Test Input Generator correctly interprets the new syntax.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct fit if the Structure Checker is modular; however, changes may ripple to the Input Generator if logic is tightly coupled to syntax.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Ensures the system remains viable as Prolog standards and dialects evolve.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator integrates PROTest II into a Continuous Integration (CI) pipeline, where the Test Report Generator produces a standardized XML output for consumption by a build server (e.g., Jenkins) after every code commit.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect fit; the current Report Generator is described as interactive and may need a new output formatter and non-interactive trigger mechanism.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Supports the requirement for full automation and integration into modern development workflows.

scenario_classification.rationale: The scenario describes the core interactive workflow the architecture was designed for, utilizing the existing sequential pipeline and user-selection capabilities.

The current architecture is described as an 'interactive' environment. Supporting a 'headless' mode requires a new automation controller to bypass user-input loops and a batch processing mechanism for directories.
The Structure Checker has 'High' change sensitivity. While modular, a new dialect requires modifying the parser and potentially the Input Generator to handle dialect-specific logic paths.
Integration with CI requires a non-interactive execution trigger and a specific XML output format, neither of which are currently native to the sequential interactive model.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No changes required; standard use case.
Implement a CLI/Automation Controller to handle directory crawling and auto-selection of predicates. Update Report Generator to support JSON serialization.
issues_identified: Interactive loops may be hard-coded into component logic
Batch processing might exceed memory constraints for large directories
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Create a wrapper script that simulates terminal input (e.g., Expect script) to drive the existing interactive tool.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Modify the Structure Checker's parser to recognize SWI-Prolog extensions. Update the Input Generator to handle any new logic constructs.
issues_identified: High change sensitivity of the Structure Checker may lead to regressions in standard Prolog parsing
Tight coupling between syntax and input generation logic
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Use an external pre-processor to transpile dialect-specific extensions into standard ISO-Prolog before analysis.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement an XML output template in the Report Generator. Add a command-line flag to the Test Driver to return non-zero exit codes on failure for CI visibility.
issues_identified: The sequential processing model may need to be wrapped to handle CI environment timeouts
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Develop a post-processor that converts the system's standard text output into JUnit-compatible XML.

scenario_interaction_weights.rationale: This component is the highest risk hotspot because it serves as the foundation for both automation (S-02) and dialect support (S-03). Evaluation findings highlight high change sensitivity and potential for regressions in standard Prolog parsing. Failure to address the tight coupling between syntax and logic here would prevent the tool from evolving to support modern Prolog extensions or batch processing.

The Test Input Generator is critically coupled with the Structure Checker. While its risk is primarily derived from upstream changes, the need to handle new logic constructs for SWI-Prolog and the potential memory constraints during batch processing (S-02) make it a significant operational bottleneck. If not addressed, the tool may produce invalid test cases for non-standard dialects.

This component is vital for the 'Automation' and 'CI/CD' business goals. While the technical risk is lower (primarily involving JSON/XML serialization), it is the primary interface for System Administrators and automated pipelines. Without these changes, the tool remains an isolated interactive utility rather than an integrated part of a modern development lifecycle.

architectural_strengths: Stable Core Logic: The architecture successfully handles standard Prolog test generation (S-01) without modification, indicating a robust foundation for its primary original use case.
Extensible Reporting: The localized effort required to add XML and JSON serialization (S-04) suggests that the Test Report Generator is modular enough to support new output formats with low risk.
Functional Decomposition: The clear separation of concerns between structure checking, input generation, and driving tests allows for targeted impact analysis across different automation scenarios.
architectural_weaknesses: Fragile Parser Coupling: The Structure Checker exhibits high change sensitivity and tight coupling with the Test Input Generator (S-03), making the system prone to regressions when supporting non-standard Prolog dialects.

UI-Logic Entanglement: Hard-coded interactive loops within component logic (S-02) represent a significant barrier to the business goal of a 'fully automated' environment, requiring widespread changes to support CLI/batch modes.

Resource and Execution Constraints: The sequential processing model and potential memory bottlenecks during batch operations (S-02, S-04) limit the architecture's suitability for high-volume CI/CD pipelines.
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: The architecture possesses moderate modifiability for peripheral features like reporting, but exhibits high rigidity in its core processing components. Significant refactoring of the parser and interaction logic is required to achieve the goal of full automation without incurring prohibitive maintenance costs.

recommendations.recommendation: Decouple the Structure Checker and Test Input Generator via a standardized Intermediate Representation (IR) or Abstract Syntax Tree (AST).
recommendations.rationale: This addresses the high-risk hotspot identified in S-03, allowing for dialect-specific extensions (like SWI-Prolog) without risking regressions in the core logic.

recommendations.recommendation: Extract interactive logic into a dedicated UI/Orchestration layer and implement a headless API for the Test Driver.
recommendations.rationale: Directly supports the strategic goal of full automation (S-02) by removing hard-coded terminal loops that currently block batch processing.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement a Strategy Pattern for the Test Report Generator to handle multiple output formats (JSON, XML, JUnit).
recommendations.rationale: Reduces the complexity of the Report Generator and makes the system more adaptable to various CI/CD toolchains as requested in S-04.

recommendations.recommendation: Introduce an external pre-processor/transpiler for non-standard Prolog dialects.
recommendations.rationale: Provides a 'quick win' for supporting SWI-Prolog (S-03) while the more complex architectural refactoring of the Structure Checker is underway.
